# PipeQuery

> A pipe-based query language for filtering, transforming, and aggregating data in JavaScript and TypeScript. Zero dependencies.

## Overview

PipeQuery (package: `@andreadito/pipequery-lang`) is a query language that chains operations with `|` pipe operators, inspired by Unix pipes and SQL. It works on arrays of objects and supports filtering, sorting, grouping, aggregation, joins, window functions, and real-time streaming queries.

- Version: 1.0.0 (beta)
- License: MIT
- Runtime: Browser + Node.js 18+
- Dependencies: Zero (core engine)
- TypeScript: Full type definitions included

## Install

```bash
npm install @andreadito/pipequery-lang
```

Requires GitHub Packages registry config: `@andreadito:registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com`

## Core API

### query(data, expression) -> unknown
Execute a query on an array or named DataContext.

```ts
import { query } from '@andreadito/pipequery-lang';
query(items, 'where(price > 100) | sort(price desc)');
query({ orders, customers }, 'orders | join(customers, customerId == id)');
```

### compile(expression) -> CompiledQuery
Pre-compile for repeated execution.

```ts
import { compile } from '@andreadito/pipequery-lang';
const fn = compile('where(price > 100) | sort(price desc)');
fn({ _data: items });
```

### liveQuery(data, expression, options) -> LiveQuery
Streaming query with incremental patch support.

```ts
import { liveQuery } from '@andreadito/pipequery-lang';
const lq = liveQuery(data, 'where(active) | sort(ts desc)', { key: 'id' });
lq.subscribe((result, stats) => { /* stats: executionMs, patchMs, rowCount */ });
lq.patch([{ id: 1, active: true }]);
lq.dispose();
```

### parseQuery(expression) -> Pipeline
Parse into AST without executing.

## Operations

| Operation | Example |
|-----------|---------|
| where(expr) | `where(price > 100)` |
| select(fields...) | `select(name, price)` |
| sort(expr dir) | `sort(price desc)` |
| groupBy(keys...) | `groupBy(category)` |
| rollup(aggs...) | `rollup(sum(price) as total)` |
| join(source, cond) | `join(orders, id == orderId)` |
| first(n) | `first(10)` |
| last(n) | `last(5)` |
| distinct(fields?) | `distinct(category)` |
| map(exprs...) | `map(price * 1.1 as newPrice)` |
| pivot(field, aggs) | `pivot(region, sum(sales))` |
| flatten(field?) | `flatten(tags)` |
| transpose(header?) | `transpose(name)` |
| reduce(init, acc) | `reduce(0, $acc + price)` |

## Aggregate Functions

Basic: sum, avg, min, max, count
Statistical: median, stddev, var, percentile, skew, kurt
Financial: vwap, wavg, drawdown, sharpe, calmar, sortino, info_ratio
Utility: distinct_count, sum_abs, first_value, last_value, pct

## Window Functions

running_sum, running_avg, running_count, running_min, running_max, row_number, lag, lead

## CLI — pq

PipeQuery ships a CLI tool (`pq`) for building data pipelines, live REST APIs, and terminal dashboards. Install globally:

```bash
npm install -g @andreadito/pq
```

### Key Feature: Instant API Endpoints

Create live JSON API endpoints on the fly — no config file edits needed:

```bash
pq init                  # scaffold a project
pq serve -d              # start server as daemon

# Create an endpoint instantly
pq endpoint add /api/top -q "crypto | sort(price desc) | first(5)"
# → http://localhost:3000/api/top is immediately available

pq endpoint add /api/summary -q "orders | groupBy(status) | count()" -c 30s
```

### Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `pq init` | Scaffold a project with example `pipequery.yaml` |
| `pq serve` | Start the server (add `-d` for daemon mode) |
| `pq stop` | Stop the running server (daemon or foreground) |
| `pq query <expr>` | Run an ad-hoc PipeQuery expression against live data |
| `pq endpoint add <path> -q <expr>` | Create a live API endpoint on the fly |
| `pq endpoint list` | List all registered endpoints |
| `pq endpoint remove <path>` | Remove an endpoint |
| `pq source add <name> -u <url>` | Add a data source at runtime |
| `pq source list` | List data sources and their health |
| `pq dashboard [-n name]` | Launch an interactive terminal dashboard |
| `pq monitor` | Live server activity monitor |

### Data Sources

Supports REST APIs, WebSockets, CSV/JSON files, and inline static data. Sources are defined in `pipequery.yaml` or added at runtime via `pq source add`. WebSocket sources support Binance-style streams (`depth`, `aggTrade`, `kline`).

### Terminal Dashboard

The `pq dashboard` command launches a TUI with:
- 7 visualization types: table, bar chart, sparkline, stat, order book, heatmap, candlestick
- Resizable 2-column grid layout (resize with `[` / `]` keys)
- Real-time SSE push updates with polling fallback
- Keyboard navigation: Tab (focus), ↑↓ (scroll), r (refresh), q (quit)

### Docker & Remote Deployment

The server and CLI are fully decoupled. Run the server in Docker and control it from anywhere:

```bash
# Run locally with Docker Desktop
docker build -t pipequery .
docker run -d -p 3000:3000 pipequery
pq remote connect http://localhost:3000

# Deploy to a remote server
pq remote deploy              # generates Dockerfile + docker-compose.yaml
pq remote connect https://my-server.example.com:3000

# All commands now work against the remote server
pq source add crypto -t rest -u "https://api.coingecko.com/..." -i 30s
pq endpoint add /api/top -q "crypto | sort(market_cap desc) | first(5)"
pq dashboard -n main
```

`pq remote connect <url>` saves the remote URL in local `pipequery.yaml`. After that, all CLI commands talk to the remote server — no local server needed.

### Server Architecture

- Fastify-based HTTP server with SSE streaming
- Hot-reload: sources, endpoints, and dashboards update without restart
- Response caching with configurable TTL
- Activity logging and health monitoring
- Daemon mode with PID management

## Sub-packages

- `@andreadito/pipequery-lang/highlighting` - CodeMirror 6 + Monaco editor support
- `@andreadito/pipequery-lang/react` - PipeQueryBuilder visual component

## Links

- Documentation: https://andreadito.github.io/pipequery/
- Repository: https://github.com/andreadito/pipequery
- Full API reference for LLMs: https://andreadito.github.io/pipequery/llms-full.txt
